design.gap: GAP D — Relation expansion (depth-1), related-field filtering (where author.name eq X), and reverse-relation includes, on both the MCP tool surface and the delivery API, with per-field publicRead AND per-collection row gates (publicFilter, access.read) fully preserved across the relation hop.
design.approach:
The design adds two library-level mechanisms and exposes them through thin, validated surface parameters. Mechanism 1 — expansion: a new `expandRelations(projectId, collection, rows, expand: string[], mode: 'full' | 'public')` in lib/entries.ts, structured exactly like the existing `resolveRelations` (lib/entries.ts:532): collect all referenced ids across the named relation fields, group by target collection, fetch each target's definition via the cached `getCollection` (lib/collections.ts:40 — no extra Neon round-trip), batch-fetch target rows in one query per target collection, then run the existing `resolveRefsForRead` on the fetched target rows so an expanded record looks exactly like a direct read of that target (its own relations as {id,label}, assets as {id,url}). The expanded value is a strict superset of today's shape: `{id, label, data: {...targetFields}}` — clients that only know {id,label} keep working. In 'public' mode (delivery), `data` is produced by the TARGET collection's `toPublicView`, target rows failing the target's `publicFilter` (checked JS-side via `matchesClauses`) silently stay in the unexpanded `{id,label}` form, and expansion of a target whose `access.read` is not 'public' or which has zero publicRead fields is rejected up front with a 422 fix hint. Depth is 1, by field name; the grammar (array of names) leaves room for dotted paths later without breaking anything. Depth-2 is deliberately not built: the embedded record carries {id,label} for its own relations, so a second hop is one targeted follow-up, while recursive expansion multiplies query fan-out and public-projection edge cases for little demonstrated need.

Mechanism 2 — related-field filtering: the where vocabulary learns dotted paths (`{field: "author.name", op: "eq", value: "X"}`), compiled inside `compileClause` (lib/query.ts:91) to a fully parameterized EXISTS subquery over an aliased entries table. `buildWhere` gains an optional related context `Map<relationFieldName, {collectionId, fields, gateClauses: WhereItem[], allowedOps: Set<WhereOp>}>` — and the gate is IN the mechanism, not bolted on at the route: the compiled subquery is `EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM entries t WHERE t.id::text = parent.data->>'author' AND t.collection_id = $targetId AND <validated clause on t.data->>'name'> AND <compiled ctx.gateClauses over t>)`. `gateClauses` are compiled via buildWhere itself against the target's FieldDef[] using a target-aliased accessor — recursion-safe because publicFilter can never contain dotted paths (field names pass NAME_RE snake_case meta-validation, and this design rejects dots in publicFilter at define time). `allowedOps` intersects with OPS_BY_TYPE on the tail field, so each surface declares its own operator policy. A new helper `collectRelatedTargets(projectId, collection, where, policy)` in lib/entries.ts scans where items for dotted fields, validates the head is a relation field, resolves targets through cached `getCollection`, and — this is the fix for the row-gate oracle — builds the context per the caller's policy: MCP policy = full OPS_BY_TYPE, empty gateClauses (MCP reads bypass publicFilter/access by documented, token-scoped design — the tool description says so out loud); DELIVERY policy = ops restricted to {eq, in}, target must have access.read 'public'/absent AND ≥1 publicRead field AND the tail field publicRead (else 422 with a hint naming the target and its filterable public fields), and gateClauses = the target's publicFilter, ANDed inside the EXISTS SQL-side. This makes delivery dotted filters observationally equivalent to "rows the caller could read via GET /v1/{target} directly" — the same rule D2/D6 state for expansion/include, closing the anonymous existence/substring oracle the review found. One hop only. Dotted paths remain legal ONLY in query contexts (query_entries/count_entries/aggregate_entries where, and delivery list filters): `compileClause` without a related context throws a targeted ValidationError, which automatically makes publicFilter, events.when, and update_entry_if.if reject dotted fields at definition/call time — so the JS mirror `matchesClauses` never needs async DB access and the buildWhere/matchesClauses parity invariant survives untouched.

Mechanism 3 — reverse relations, which fall out of the same table shape: `includeReverse: [{collection, field, limit?}]` on query_entries/get_entry fetches children in ONE window-function statement per spec (`row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY data->>'field' ORDER BY created_at, id)` with `rn <= limit+1`, giving exact per-parent hasMore), and attaches them as a SIBLING key `related["<collection>.<field>"] = {entries, hasMore}` next to `data` — never merged into `data`, so resolved/expanded rows can never be mistaken for storable entry payloads. On delivery the same child gates as expansion apply (child read must be public, child publicFilter enforced inside the SQL via the existing `buildWhere`, output through child `toPublicView`). Everything is read-only, single-statement SQL compatible with the neon-http driver, host-agnostic, and requires zero schema migrations.

Fit with AgentX: every piece reuses an existing seam the codebase already names — the batched resolve pattern, the cached collection registry, the validated where compiler, the toPublicView projection choke point, and the three-step tool-addition recipe (TOOL_DEFS + zod parse + error code). The one rule an agent can state for every hop feature on delivery is uniform: expansion, include, AND dotted filters never reveal anything (field values OR row existence) that the target collection's own public endpoint wouldn't. Delivery self-description updates ride the existing `get_project_info.deliveryApi` strings so an agent discovers ?expand and dotted filters without documentation.

keyDecisions.decision: Depth-1 expansion only, by relation-field name (expand: ["author"]); expanded value shape is {id, label, data} — a superset of today's {id,label}
keyDecisions.rationale: Kills the N+1 for the dominant case (post→author, order→customer) with bounded cost (2 batched queries per distinct target collection). Embedded records carry {id,label} for their own relations, so any second hop is one targeted follow-up call. The array-of-names grammar upgrades cleanly to dotted paths if depth-2 is ever justified, and the superset shape means existing consumers of {id,label} never break.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Recursive depth-2 expansion — multiplies query fan-out per hop, forces publicRead/publicFilter/access checks across a graph of collections in one response, and complicates cycle handling (author→posts→author), all for marginal value.

keyDecisions.decision: On delivery, an expanded record shows exactly what a direct GET of the target would show: target toPublicView projection, target publicFilter row gate (non-matching rows stay {id,label}), and target access.read must be 'public'
keyDecisions.rationale: One rule an agent can state and rely on: expansion never reveals anything the target's own endpoint wouldn't. The label already leaks by existing design (resolveRelations runs regardless of target gates), so falling back to {id,label} for gated rows adds zero new exposure.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Embedding raw target.data (leaks private fields — violates the per-field publicRead invariant) or 404ing the whole parent when a related row is gated (leaks existence of hidden rows and breaks list responses unpredictably).

keyDecisions.decision: Delivery dotted filters are gated on the TARGET exactly like expansion (D2) and include (D6): target access.read must be 'public'/absent and have ≥1 publicRead field (422 with hint otherwise), the tail field must be publicRead, AND the target's publicFilter clauses are compiled via buildWhere and ANDed INSIDE the EXISTS subquery — the gate lives in the compiled SQL, not as an afterthought at the route
keyDecisions.rationale: This closes the reviewers' blocker: without it, `?author.email=victim@example.com` against an owner-gated users collection is an anonymous yes/no oracle over row-gated data, violating the codebase's own 'filtering on a private field leaks contents through result differences' defense (route.ts:74) — and even a fully public target would leak rows its publicFilter hides. Row gates (publicFilter + access.read), not field-level publicRead alone, define what delivery may observe about a collection; putting gateClauses in the related-context mechanism makes it impossible for a future call site to compile a delivery dotted filter without them (missing gate context on the delivery policy path is a construction error, not a forgotten check). Recursion is impossible: publicFilter cannot contain dotted paths (rejected at define time; pre-existing filters can't contain '.' because field names pass NAME_RE).
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Field-level-only validation (publicRead on both hops), as the original design had — it treats publicRead as the gate for row-gated collections, which it is not; also rejected: silently dropping the dotted clause for gated targets (violates rule 2 — the agent learns nothing) and evaluating the gate JS-side post-fetch (the leak is through result differences in the parent list, so it must be inside the SQL).

keyDecisions.decision: Dotted-path operators are policy-scoped per surface: delivery allows {eq, in} only (the URL param loop is eq-only anyway; the whitelist is enforced structurally in the related context so a future where-grammar exposure can't widen it silently); MCP keeps the full OPS_BY_TYPE including contains — the reviewers' suggestion to drop contains on MCP is not adopted
keyDecisions.rationale: On delivery, contains across a hop is a substring-extraction oracle (binary-search field contents via repeated prefix probes), so equality-class ops are the correct ceiling even WITH the row-gate fix — defense in depth. On MCP the threat model is different and the restriction would be security theater: an mcp-scoped token already has unrestricted read over every collection in the project (MCP reads bypass publicFilter and publicRead by documented design), so `where author.bio contains X` reveals nothing the same caller can't get with a direct `query_entries` on the target. Removing contains there would only degrade query power without removing any capability. The boundary is stated in both tool descriptions ('delivery dotted filters: eq only; MCP dotted filters bypass publicFilter/access like all MCP reads').
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Dropping contains on dotted paths everywhere including MCP (blocks legitimate agent queries while the same agent can trivially reconstruct the result with two calls — no security gained) or allowing contains on delivery behind the row-gate fix (still amplifies any future gate bug from existence-probe to content recovery; eq/in covers the real delivery use cases).

keyDecisions.decision: Related-field filters compile to a parameterized EXISTS subquery with an aliased entries table, matching on t.id::text = parent.data->>'rel', with tail-field/op validation against the target schema, surface-scoped allowedOps, and gateClauses all resolved through the same context; one hop only
keyDecisions.rationale: EXISTS is a single statement (neon-http compatible), composes with the existing conditions array in queryEntriesPage/countEntries/aggregateEntries without touching pagination's total order, and every identifier in it is meta-validated — the where language stays a closed vocabulary. The ::text comparison avoids uuid-cast exceptions on absent/null relation values. Carrying gateClauses and allowedOps in the same context object keeps policy and mechanism in one validated structure.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: LATERAL joins (perturb the select shape and cursor/orderBy total-order guarantees in the drizzle builder) and denormalizing target labels into parent rows (breaks single-source-of-truth on target updates, adds write-path fan-out).

keyDecisions.decision: Dotted where paths are legal only in query contexts; publicFilter, events.when, and update_entry_if.if reject them with a targeted fix hint (compileClause throws when no related-schema context is supplied)
keyDecisions.rationale: matchesClauses is the synchronous JS mirror used for single-entry row gates and event when-clauses — it cannot do DB lookups. Restricting dotted paths keeps the documented buildWhere ≡ matchesClauses parity invariant intact for every context where both run, and the restriction is enforced structurally (missing context ⇒ ValidationError) rather than by convention. It is also what makes compiling target publicFilters inside the EXISTS recursion-safe.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Making matchesClauses async and DB-aware so gates/when-clauses support related fields — spreads DB access into the event emit path and the single-entry 404 gate, and turns a cheap in-memory check into per-request round-trips.

keyDecisions.decision: Reverse relations are a separate includeReverse parameter returning a sibling `related` object on each entry, fetched via one window-function statement per spec with exact per-parent hasMore
keyDecisions.rationale: Keying children outside `data` prevents field-name collisions and preserves the discipline that resolved rows are never re-validated or re-stored; row_number() PARTITION BY gives per-parent limits and exact hasMore (limit+1 idiom) in a single statement the HTTP driver can run.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Overloading the expand grammar for reverse traversal (conflates a cheap O(ids) lookup with an unbounded child scan, and reverse results are lists with their own pagination semantics, not scalar embeds) or N-per-parent queries (reintroduces the N+1 this gap exists to kill).

keyDecisions.decision: select + expand conflict is an explicit E_VALIDATION error ('add "author" to select or drop it from expand'), not an implicit include
keyDecisions.rationale: The code applies select BEFORE ref resolution precisely so unselected relations cost nothing; silently expanding an unselected field would violate select's contract. An explicit error with the fix in the message is exactly design rule 2.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Implicitly adding expanded fields to the selection — surprising payloads, and the agent learns nothing about the interaction.

increments.name: MCP depth-1 expand on query_entries + get_entry
increments.what: Add expandRelations(projectId, collection, rows, expand, mode) to lib/entries.ts, modeled on resolveRelations: validate each expand name is a relation field (error lists expandable fields), resolve target collections via cached getCollection, one batched entries fetch per distinct target collection, run resolveRefsForRead on target rows, rewrite parent values to {id, label, data}. Wire `expand?: string[]` into queryArgs and the get_entry zod schema in lib/mcp/tools.ts; in query_entries, run expansion after the existing resolveRefsForRead (expanded fields skip the plain label pass), and reject expand names excluded by select with a fix-hint error. Update both tools' TOOL_DEFS inputSchema + description ('depth 1 only; expanded value is {id,label,data} with the target's own relations as {id,label}'). Smoke test: define authors+posts, expand author on query_entries and get_entry, assert embedded data and unexpanded fallback for null relations.
increments.surface: MCP tools query_entries + get_entry gain `expand: string[]`; descriptions state the depth-1 boundary

increments.name: Delivery ?expand= with target public projection and row gates
increments.what: Expose ?expand=author,category on GET /api/v1/{collection} and /{collection}/{id}. Add 'expand' to the reserved-param skip list (route.ts:78). Validate: each name must be a publicRead relation field on the parent (422 listing expandable public fields); target collection must have >=1 publicRead field and access.read 'public'/absent (422 with hint naming the target). Call expandRelations in 'public' mode: target rows projected via the TARGET's toPublicView, rows failing target publicFilter (matchesClauses) stay {id,label}. Run expansion before the parent's toPublicView so private parent relations can never be expanded. Update get_project_info deliveryApi.read string in lib/mcp/tools.ts to advertise ?expand. Smoke: expanded public GET shows only target public fields; gated target row stays {id,label}; expand on non-public relation 422s.
increments.surface: Delivery API: ?expand= on list + single GET; get_project_info self-description updated; errors as {error, code} 422

increments.name: Related-field where (dotted paths) on MCP query/count/aggregate, with policy-scoped context
increments.what: Extend lib/query.ts: buildWhere/compileClause accept an optional related context Map<relationField, {collectionId, fields, gateClauses: WhereItem[], allowedOps: Set<WhereOp>}>; a clause whose field contains '.' splits into (relationField, targetField) — head must be a relation field, tail validated against the target's FieldDef[] with OPS_BY_TYPE intersected with ctx.allowedOps (op outside the surface's whitelist gets a targeted E_VALIDATION naming the allowed ops), then compiled to EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM alias(entries) t WHERE t.id::text = parent.data->>head AND t.collection_id = ctx.collectionId AND <clause over t.data->>tail> AND <buildWhere(ctx.gateClauses, ctx.fields) compiled against alias t>) using drizzle's alias() and a target-aware accessor (accessor gains an optional table-alias argument). gateClauses are themselves compiled through buildWhere with NO related context, so a dotted field inside them throws — structural recursion safety. Without any context, dotted fields throw 'related-field clauses are only valid in query where — not publicFilter/events.when/if'. More than one hop throws with a hint. Add collectRelatedTargets(projectId, collection, where, policy: 'mcp' | 'delivery') in lib/entries.ts (cached getCollection per distinct relation head): 'mcp' policy sets allowedOps = full OPS_BY_TYPE and gateClauses = [] (MCP reads bypass publicFilter/access by documented design); 'delivery' policy is consumed by D4. Thread the context through queryEntriesPage, countEntries, and aggregateEntries opts. Update query_entries/count_entries/aggregate_entries descriptions and WHERE_CLAUSE_JSON field description in lib/mcp/tools.ts ('field may be "relationField.targetField", one hop; ops type-checked against the target field; on MCP, dotted filters read the target like any MCP read — publicFilter/access do not apply'). Smoke: where author.name eq X returns only matching posts; contains across the hop works on MCP; dotted field in publicFilter/update_entry_if.if rejected with the hint; op mismatch on target type rejected; a dotted field inside a hypothetical gateClauses input rejected (unit-level).
increments.surface: MCP where vocabulary on query_entries/count_entries/aggregate_entries gains dotted paths (full OPS_BY_TYPE, documented publicFilter/access bypass consistent with all MCP reads); publicFilter/events.when/update_entry_if.if explicitly reject them; related context carries per-surface allowedOps + gateClauses

increments.name: Delivery related-field eq filter (?author.name=X) with full target row gates
increments.what: In the GET list param loop (app/api/v1/[collection]/route.ts:77), keys containing '.' are parsed as related filters. Validation, all before any query runs, each failing with a 422 fix hint: (1) the relation head must be a publicRead relation field on the parent (extend the existing 'unknown or non-public filter field' message to list dotted options); (2) the TARGET collection must have access.read 'public'/absent AND >=1 publicRead field — same gate as D2 expansion, hint names the target and why ('target collection authors is not publicly readable; related filters are only allowed against targets you could GET directly'); (3) the tail must be a publicRead field on the target; (4) operator is eq only (coerceParam runs against the TARGET field's type) — the delivery policy in collectRelatedTargets sets allowedOps={eq,in} so even a future richer delivery where-grammar cannot widen this without touching the policy. The delivery policy also sets gateClauses = the target's publicFilter (or []), which D3's compiler ANDs INSIDE the EXISTS — so a parent row matches only if its related row would be visible via GET /v1/{target}. The clause joins effectiveWhere and compiles through the D3 machinery. Update the deliveryApi.read self-description string ('?relation.field=value equality filters; target must be publicly readable; target row visibility rules apply'). Smoke (security assertions, all three reviewer scenarios): (a) anonymous ?author.email=x against an access.read='owner' users target 422s — no existence oracle; (b) target authors public with publicFilter [{approved eq true}]: a post whose author is unapproved does NOT match ?author.name=Alice (no leak through result differences), while an approved author's posts do; (c) dotted filter on a non-publicRead tail field 422s; (d) contains-style op is unreachable from the URL surface (eq-only loop) and the policy whitelist rejects in/gt/etc. injected at unit level.
increments.surface: Delivery API: ?relationField.targetField=value equality filters on GET list, gated by target access.read + publicRead + publicFilter-inside-EXISTS; self-description states the target-visibility rule

increments.name: MCP reverse-relation include (children of parents)
increments.what: Add includeReverse to query_entries and get_entry: [{collection, field, limit? (default 20, max 100)}], max 3 specs. Validate: named collection exists, named field is a relation field on it targeting the queried collection (error lists valid reverse paths). Fetch per spec in ONE statement: SELECT ... row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY data->>field ORDER BY date_trunc('milliseconds', created_at), id) rn ... WHERE collection_id = child.id AND data->>field IN (parent ids) with rn <= limit+1 — exact per-parent hasMore via the limit+1 idiom. Attach as a sibling key on each result: related: {"comments.post": {entries: [{id, data}], hasMore}} — never inside data. Child rows get resolveRefsForRead. Update TOOL_DEFS schema + description (boundary: reverse children are capped per parent; page deeper via a direct query_entries where child.field eq parentId).
increments.surface: MCP query_entries + get_entry gain includeReverse; results carry a `related` sibling object per entry

increments.name: Delivery reverse include (?include=comments.post)
increments.what: Expose ?include=childCollection.relationField (comma-separated, max 3) on delivery GET list + single, reusing D5's fetch with D2's gate pattern: child collection must have >=1 publicRead field and access.read 'public'/absent (422 with hint); child publicFilter clauses are ANDed into the child fetch's WHERE via buildWhere (SQL-side, not post-filtered); children projected through the child's toPublicView. Add 'include' to the reserved-param skip list. Fixed child limit 20 per parent with hasMore. Update deliveryApi.read self-description. Smoke: public parent list embeds only public child fields, respects child publicFilter, 422 on non-public child.
increments.surface: Delivery API: ?include= reverse embeds on GET list + single

design.invariantCompliance: Rule 1 (declarative + self-describing): every new capability is a parameter on existing tools/endpoints, documented in TOOL_DEFS descriptions (both zod parse schema and hand-written JSON inputSchema updated together, per the known drift gotcha) and in get_project_info's deliveryApi strings; boundaries stated out loud — 'expand is depth 1', 'dotted where paths are one hop and query-only', 'MCP dotted filters read the target like any MCP read (publicFilter/access do not apply)', 'delivery dotted filters: eq only, target must be publicly readable, target row visibility applies', 'no ordering by related fields', 'reverse children capped per parent'. Rule 2 (machine-readable errors): every rejection is a ValidationError/422 whose message contains the repair — expandable field lists, filterable dotted options, allowed-op lists on op-whitelist misses, the exact 'add to select or drop from expand' fix, 'related-field clauses are only valid in query where', and the delivery target-gate hint stating WHY ('related filters are only allowed against targets you could GET directly'); no new error codes needed (E_VALIDATION covers all), keeping ERROR_CODES append-only. Rule 3 (secrets as references): no secrets are involved anywhere in this gap. Rule 4 (plan + confirm): all six increments are read-only — no destructive surface, so no confirm gates needed; nothing weakens the existing define/delete plans. Rule 5 (strict validation never weakens) — now covering BOTH projection and row gates across the hop: (a) field-level — expansion output on delivery passes through the TARGET collection's toPublicView, and delivery dotted filters require publicRead on head, tail, and the target having >=1 publicRead field, preserving per-field publicRead transitively; data still never leaves except through toPublicView. (b) Row-level — the 'filtering on a private field leaks contents through result differences' defense (route.ts:74) is extended across the hop: delivery dotted filters 422 unless the target's access.read is 'public'/absent, and the target's publicFilter is compiled INTO the EXISTS subquery, so no parent result difference can ever reveal the existence or content of a target row the caller couldn't read via GET /v1/{target} directly — the same one-rule guarantee D2/D6 give expansion/include. The gate lives in the related-context mechanism (gateClauses + allowedOps), so it cannot be forgotten at a call site. (c) Operator containment — delivery dotted ops are whitelisted to {eq,in} structurally, eliminating the contains substring-extraction oracle even as defense-in-depth on top of the row gate; MCP keeps contains because an mcp-scoped token already has unrestricted project read (no privilege is crossed). (d) Compilation discipline — related-field filters use only meta-validated field names against OPS_BY_TYPE with all values parameterized; gateClauses compile through buildWhere with no related context so recursion is structurally impossible (and publicFilter can't contain dots anyway — NAME_RE); the EXISTS subquery introduces zero sql.raw of user input. (e) The buildWhere ≡ matchesClauses parity invariant is preserved structurally by rejecting dotted paths in every context where the JS mirror runs; expanded/reverse data lives in resolved shapes ({id,label,data} / sibling `related`) that are never re-validated or re-stored.
crossGapDependencies: None required — this gap is read-path only and uses no queue/cron/streaming machinery.

Coordination note for any gap extending the where vocabulary (e.g. a change-feed gap whitelisting an updatedAt pseudo-field): the '.' character in clause field names is now reserved for relation paths — pseudo-fields must not contain dots.

Coordination note for the delivery cursor-pagination change (query-delivery extension point): ?after/?expand/?include must all live in the same reserved-param skip list at app/api/v1/[collection]/route.ts:78 — whichever lands second must extend, not replace, the list.

Coordination note for any future 'authenticated/owner-aware delivery filtering' gap: the delivery dotted-filter policy currently hard-422s non-public targets even for authenticated callers; relaxing that (e.g. ANDing an ownerClause for the target into the EXISTS instead of rejecting) must go through the same gateClauses mechanism, never by dropping the gate.
risks: EXISTS matching on t.id::text = data->>'rel' cannot use the entries primary-key index directly; per-candidate-row child scans are fine at current collection sizes but degrade on large child tables — mitigation path is an expression index on (data->>'<field>') for hot relation fields, following the existing entries_uq_ partial-index DDL pattern (meta-validated names only). Defer until measured. The publicFilter clauses now ANDed inside the EXISTS add predicate cost but not join cost.

matchesClauses/buildWhere are parallel implementations; the design keeps parity by construction (dotted paths structurally rejected where the JS mirror runs), but a future contributor allowing dotted paths in publicFilter without touching matchesClauses would silently 404 single-entry reads — worth a comment at both sites plus the smoke test asserting the rejection (in D3's suite).

Target-gate policy drift: D2 (expand), D4 (dotted filter), and D6 (include) each state the same 'target must be publicly readable' rule — if the checks are written three times they can diverge. Mitigation: factor one assertPublicTarget(targetCollection) helper (validates access.read + publicRead count, returns publicFilter for gateClauses) shared by all three delivery paths, so the rule has one implementation.

Cached-collection staleness: the target's publicFilter/access used in the EXISTS come from unstable_cache'd getCollection; within the tag-revalidation window a just-tightened filter could briefly not apply. This is the same staleness bound as direct reads of the target collection today (define writes revalidate the tag), so no new exposure class — but the known Render-move cache replacement must preserve the invalidation-on-define behavior or the window grows.

Expansion multiplies cached getCollection calls (one per distinct target collection per request); harmless today via unstable_cache, but the known Render-move cache replacement must land before this becomes N Neon round-trips per read.

Window-function reverse query is a single SELECT and should run over the neon-http driver, but this is exactly the class the infra map flags as 'verify' — D5's smoke test must run against a real Neon branch before ship, not just local pg.

Response payload growth: expand + include can inflate list bodies substantially (500 parents x 20 children); ETag hashing and JSON serialization costs grow linearly — the existing silent MAX_QUERY_LIMIT clamp plus the per-parent child caps bound it, but delivery consumers on slow links may need the caps documented prominently in get_project_info.

TOOL_DEFS JSON schema vs zod drift (known gotcha) now has three more touch points (expand, dotted-field description incl. the MCP-bypass and delivery-gate boundary text, includeReverse) — each increment's checklist must update both representations.

Behavioral coupling for API consumers: tightening a target collection's access.read or publicFilter after clients depend on ?author.name= filters will start 422ing (gate) or shrinking results (filter-in-EXISTS). This is correct — visibility rules must win — but the deliveryApi self-description should state that related filters inherit the target's visibility so agents aren't surprised.

openMinor.issue: D6's delivery response shape is under-specified and the D5 'sibling related key' framing doesn't transfer: MCP entries are {id, data} (so a sibling `related` is unambiguous), but delivery views are FLAT — toPublicView produces {id, ...publicFields} spread at the top level (route.ts:111-117). 'related' is a legal field name under NAME_RE with no reserved-field-name list, so a publicRead field literally named `related` would collide with the embedded reverse-include object, silently clobbering one or the other. An implementer hits this ambiguity immediately in D6.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Specify the delivery embed shape explicitly in D6 (e.g. nest under a single `related` key AND either add 'related' to a new reserved-field-name check in fieldDefSchema, or 422 the ?include request when the parent has a public field named 'related' with a rename hint). State the chosen rule in the deliveryApi self-description string.

openMinor.issue: get_client_code is forgotten. The mcp-surface map explicitly flags that its output is a schema snapshot that 'must be regenerated after any define_collection change' and it generates delivery-consumption code. D2/D4/D6 add ?expand, dotted filters, and ?include to the delivery API and update get_project_info's deliveryApi strings, but no increment updates the get_client_code generator — agents pulling client code get a surface that silently lacks the new query power, violating rule 1 (every capability visible through the tool surface).
openMinor.suggestedFix: Add the get_client_code template/generator (in lib/mcp/tools.ts, already in filesTouched) to D2, D4, and D6's work items, with a smoke assertion that generated code mentions expand/include when the schema has relations.

openMinor.issue: D5 (MCP reverse include) delivers less new value than its M-size suggests: on the MCP surface, 'children of a page of parents' is already expressible today in ONE existing call — query_entries on the child collection with {field: 'post', op: 'in', value: [parentIds]} (OPS_BY_TYPE allows 'in' on relation fields, lib/query.ts:44). The only genuinely new capability in D5 is per-parent capping/hasMore via the window function. The real N+1 gap is on DELIVERY, where URL filters are eq-only, yet D6 depends on building the heavier MCP machinery first.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Consider splitting differently: (a) a near-free increment documenting the op:'in' child-fetch recipe in query_entries/get_client_code descriptions (rule 1 self-description), and (b) evaluate whether the window-function per-parent capping is needed at all before building D5/D6 — or scope reverse includes to delivery only, where no equivalent exists.

openMinor.issue: D2's stated rule and its mechanism disagree for authenticated readers: the keyDecision says 'expansion never reveals anything the target's own endpoint wouldn't', but the mechanism rejects expansion outright unless target access.read is 'public'/absent. An authenticated user whose X-User-Token would pass a direct GET of an access.read='authenticated' target cannot expand it — stricter than the stated rule. Not a security hole (conservative direction), but the boundary an agent reads in the tool description won't match observed behavior.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Either restate the boundary honestly ('expansion only for public-read targets in v1') in the 422 hint and deliveryApi strings, or pass the verified user through the target's gateRead when deciding expandability. The former is the cheap, shippable choice; just make the description and the rationale say the same thing.
